questions.q: Minimum age for a Private Pilot Certificate (airplane)?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.103(a): Must be at least 17 years old. Solo privileges require only 16.

questions.q: Total flight hours required for PPL under Part 61?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.109(a): Minimum 40 total flight hours. Part 141 approved courses may require as few as 35 hours.

questions.q: Bottle-to-throttle minimum time after consuming alcohol?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.17(a)(2): No crewmember may act within 8 hours of consuming alcohol.

questions.q: To carry passengers, how many T&Ls in the preceding 90 days?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.107(a)(3): 3 takeoffs and 3 landings within the preceding 90 days in the same category and class.

questions.q: AROW — required aircraft documents on board?
choices: Airworthiness, Registration, Operating handbook, Weight and balance
questions.explanation: AROW: Airworthiness certificate, Registration, Operating handbook/AFM, Weight and balance data.

questions.q: How often must a flight review (BFR) be completed?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.56: Flight review required every 24 calendar months — at least 1 hr ground plus 1 hr flight.

questions.q: Maximum airspeed below 10,000 ft MSL?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.117(a): No more than 250 KIAS below 10,000 ft MSL.

questions.q: Solo flight hours required in the make/model within 60 days of checkride?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.109(a)(6): At least 3 hours of solo flight in the make and model within 60 days preceding the practical test.

questions.q: Documents a pilot must carry when acting as PIC?
choices: Pilot certificate, valid medical, government-issued photo ID
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.3: Pilot certificate, valid medical certificate or BasicMed, and government-issued photo ID.

questions.q: Maximum BAC allowed when acting as crewmember?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.17(a)(4): Blood alcohol concentration must be less than 0.04 percent.

questions.q: Expense sharing with passengers — what must the pilot pay at minimum?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.113(b): Pilot must pay at least their equal share. With 3 passengers, each person pays one-quarter.

questions.q: 3rd-class medical validity for a 38-year-old pilot?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.23(d): 3rd-class medical valid 60 calendar months for pilots under 40; 24 months for pilots 40 and older.

questions.q: Required solo cross-country total distance for PPL?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.109(a)(5): Solo XC must be at least 150 nm total, full-stop at 3 or more points, one leg 50 nm or more.

questions.q: Night currency time window for carrying passengers?
choices: 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise
30 minutes after sunset to 30 minutes before sunrise
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.107(a)(3)(ii): Night period is defined as 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise.

questions.q: Annual inspection completed March 15 — when does it expire?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.409: Expires at the end of the 12th calendar month — March 31 of the following year.

questions.q: Who is responsible for determining aircraft airworthiness per 91.7?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.7(b): The PIC is responsible for determining whether the aircraft is in condition for safe flight.

questions.q: Minimum altitude over a congested area?
choices: 500 ft above highest obstacle within 2000 ft
1000 ft above highest obstacle within 2000 ft horizontal
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.119(b): Over congested areas, 1000 ft above the highest obstacle within a 2000 ft horizontal radius.

questions.q: Special VFR minimum visibility in Class D airspace?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.157: Special VFR clearances require at least 1 statute mile flight visibility and clear of clouds.

questions.q: Night defined for logging flight time (14 CFR 61.51)?
choices: End of evening civil twilight to beginning of morning civil twilight
1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.51(b)(3)(i): Night equals end of evening civil twilight to beginning of morning civil twilight.

questions.q: When overtaking another aircraft, which side do you pass on?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.113(f): The overtaking aircraft alters course to the RIGHT to pass well clear.

questions.q: Two aircraft converging at same altitude — who has right of way?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.113(d): When aircraft converge, the aircraft on the RIGHT has right-of-way.

questions.q: Right-of-way hierarchy — balloon vs glider vs powered aircraft?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.113(b): After aircraft in distress, order is balloon, glider, airship, aircraft towing, then powered aircraft.

questions.q: Inoperative transponder discovered before flight — conditions for legal departure?
choices: With ATC authorization, avoiding airspace requiring transponder
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.215(d): ATC may authorize a deviation. Flight must avoid Class A, B, C airspace and the Mode C veil.

questions.q: Required IFR inspections beyond the annual (most complete)?
choices: Annual plus altimeter/static 24 mo, transponder 24 mo, VOR 30 days
questions.explanation: For IFR: altimeter/pitot-static (91.411, 24 cal months), transponder (91.413, 24 cal months), VOR check (91.171, 30 days).

questions.q: Process for flying with inoperative equipment under 91.213 with no MEL?
choices: Verify not required by type cert or ops rules, deactivate, placard INOP, mechanic confirms safe
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.213: If not required by type certificate or operating rules, deactivate, placard INOP, and a certificated mechanic determines no safety impact.

questions.q: Student pilot solo XC — what endorsements are required?
choices: Both logbook AND certificate endorsement for specific route or area
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.93: Requires both a logbook endorsement and a student pilot certificate endorsement for the make, model, and route.

questions.q: PIC authority to deviate from FARs in an emergency?
choices: May deviate as necessary; submit report to FAA if requested
May deviate only from weather minimums
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.3(b): In an emergency, PIC may deviate from any FAR to the extent necessary. Written report may be required.

questions.q: An AD requires action within 50 flight hours; you are at 48 hours. When must you comply?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 39: ADs are legally enforceable. Must comply before reaching the 50-hour threshold — within the next 2 flight hours.

questions.q: Presidential TFR under 91.141 — inner ring restriction?
choices: All civilian flight; outer ring requires ATC coordination
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.141: Inner ring (typically 10 nm, surface to FL180) prohibits civilian flight. Outer ring (30 nm) requires ATC coordination.

questions.q: ATC issues an instruction you believe would create a safety hazard. Your authority?
choices: Must comply — ATC has final authority
Must comply unless you declare an emergency first
You have final authority under 91.3; may deviate and notify ATC
Ask for a supervisor on frequency
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.3(a): The PIC is the final authority regarding the operation of the aircraft. In an emergency, 91.3(b) allows deviation from any rule.

questions.q: Under 14 CFR 91.409, a 100-hour inspection is required for aircraft used for what purpose?
choices: Any aircraft flown for personal use
Aircraft used to carry persons for hire or flight instruction for hire
All certificated aircraft regardless of use
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.409(b): A 100-hour inspection is required for aircraft used to carry persons for hire or used for flight instruction for hire. It is in addition to, not a replacement for, the annual inspection.

questions.q: Can a 100-hour inspection be exceeded? If so, by how much?
choices: Yes — up to 10 hours to fly to a location where the inspection can be performed
Yes — up to 5 hours to fly to a location where the inspection can be performed
Yes — any amount if the pilot accepts responsibility
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.409(b): The 100-hour limitation may be exceeded by not more than 10 hours if necessary to fly the aircraft to a place where the inspection can be done. The excess time must be included in computing the next 100 hours.

questions.q: A pilot flies an aircraft that requires a type rating without holding that type rating. What regulation applies?
choices: 14 CFR 61.31 — a type rating is required to act as PIC of certain large aircraft, turbojet-powered aircraft, or as specified by the FAA
No regulation — type ratings are optional
14 CFR 61.56 — only the flight review matters
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.31: Type ratings are required for aircraft over 12,500 lbs MGTOW, turbojet-powered aircraft, and other specific aircraft designated by the FAA. Operating without the required type rating is a certificate action offense.

questions.q: What is the minimum visibility and ceiling for a student pilot to conduct a solo cross-country flight?
choices: The visibility and ceiling must meet VFR minimums for the airspace AND meet the instructor-set personal minimums in the student's logbook endorsement
Only VFR minimums apply — same as any VFR pilot
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.93 and 61.87: The flight instructor must provide a solo XC endorsement and may set specific weather minimums in the endorsement. The student may not fly if conditions are below either VFR minimums OR the instructor's stated minimums.

questions.q: What does BasicMed allow a private pilot to do that a regular 3rd-class medical does not simplify?
choices: Allows flying without any medical review
Allows private pilots to act as PIC using a regular medical examination by any state-licensed physician rather than an FAA Aviation Medical Examiner — valid 48 months
Allows instrument flight without any medical
Replaces the need for a flight review
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.23 and AC 68-1: BasicMed allows pilots to get a medical examination from any state-licensed physician (not necessarily an AME). The exam is valid 48 calendar months and requires completion of an online course every 24 months.

questions.q: A pilot has a student pilot certificate. What endorsement is needed before the first solo flight?
choices: No endorsement needed — certificate is sufficient
An endorsement in the student's logbook from their instructor authorizing solo flight in a specific make and model of aircraft
Only a medical certificate is required
A written test must be passed first
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.87(n): Before solo flight, a student pilot must have an instructor endorsement in their logbook that specifically authorizes solo in the make and model of aircraft to be flown.

questions.q: When is a pre-solo written test required and who administers it?
choices: Never — the practical test standards require only an oral evaluation; no written test is mandated for solo flight, only for the private pilot certificate knowledge test administered at an FAA-approved testing center
Before the first solo flight — the student must pass a written test on the applicable regulations and the flight characteristics of the aircraft, administered and graded by the student's instructor

Before the first solo cross-country flight — the student must pass a written test on cross-country navigation procedures and the performance limitations of the aircraft, administered and graded by a designated pilot examiner

Before the private pilot checkride only — the student must pass a written test on the applicable regulations, administered and graded by an FAA inspector or designated pilot examiner at an approved knowledge testing center
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.87(b): Before solo flight, an instructor must administer and grade a written test covering Part 91 rules applicable to student solo flight and the flight characteristics and limitations of the aircraft.

questions.q: Under 14 CFR 91.111, a pilot must not operate so close to another aircraft as to create what?
choices: Turbulence that affects the other aircraft
Noise in excess of EPA limits
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.111(a): No person may operate an aircraft so close to another aircraft as to create a collision hazard.

questions.q: What does 14 CFR 91.151 require regarding VFR fuel reserves?
choices: Only enough fuel to reach destination
Day: enough fuel to destination plus 30 minutes at cruise. Night: enough fuel to destination plus 45 minutes at cruise.
Always carry enough fuel for 1 hour beyond destination
Fuel reserves are only required for IFR flight
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.151(a): VFR day requires fuel to reach first intended landing plus 30 minutes at normal cruise. VFR night requires fuel to reach first intended landing plus 45 minutes at normal cruise.

questions.q: What is a logbook endorsement and what is its legal significance?
choices: A social endorsement — has no legal standing
A written statement by a certificated flight instructor in the pilot's logbook authorizing specific operations — legally required for solo, solo XC, and practical tests
Required only after a failed checkride
questions.explanation: Endorsements by a CFI in a pilot's logbook carry legal weight under 14 CFR Part 61. They certify that training requirements have been met and authorize specific operations. Missing required endorsements can be grounds for certificate action.

questions.q: Under 14 CFR 91.119, what altitude must be maintained over open water or sparsely populated areas?
choices: 500 ft above the surface, except when necessary for takeoff and landing
1000 ft above the highest obstacle within 2000 ft horizontal
No specific requirement — pilot judgment applies
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.119(c): Over open water or sparsely populated areas, the pilot must not operate below 500 ft above the surface except when necessary for takeoff or landing.

questions.q: What is an ELT and when must its battery be replaced?
choices: Emergency Landing Transponder — replaced at every annual
Emergency Locator Transmitter — battery must be replaced when 50% of its useful life has expired OR after 1 cumulative hour of use, whichever comes first
Emergency Locator Transmitter — replaced every 2 years regardless
ELTs do not have replacement requirements
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.207(c): ELT batteries must be replaced when 50% of their useful life (or useful life of charge) has expired, or after the ELT has been in use for more than 1 cumulative hour. Replacement date must be marked on the outside of the transmitter.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the privileges of a private pilot certificate?
choices: Acting as PIC for pleasure flights
Carrying passengers for compensation when splitting fuel costs equally
Acting as PIC on a revenue-generating charter flight
Flying in Class D airspace with required equipment
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.113(a): Private pilots may not act as PIC of an aircraft for compensation or hire. Revenue-generating charter operations require at minimum a commercial pilot certificate.

questions.q: What is the difference between a safety pilot and a safety pilot acting as PIC?
choices: There is no legal distinction — the safety pilot always occupies the PIC seat, logs all flight time as PIC, and must meet all currency requirements including flight reviews and medical certificates for the class of airspace

A safety pilot may act as required crewmember (not PIC) when the flying pilot maintains PIC status, OR the safety pilot may be designated PIC before the flight begins — this affects who must meet currency and medical requirements

A safety pilot must always be designated PIC before flight — logging all flight time as PIC while the pilot under the foggles logs simulated instrument time as SIC — this division is required by currency rules for both crew positions

A safety pilot may substitute for a flight instructor during simulated instrument flight when the flying pilot holds an instrument rating — this arrangement affects only the instrument currency logging, not medical or flight review requirements
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.109(c): A safety pilot must at minimum hold a private certificate and appropriate medical. If the safety pilot is the required PIC, they log PIC time and must be current. If the flying pilot is PIC, they log PIC and the safety pilot logs SIC. This must be agreed upon before flight.

questions.q: What does 14 CFR 91.13 prohibit?
choices: Operating an aircraft in a careless or reckless manner so as to endanger the life or property of another
Flying at night without position lights
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.13(a): No person may operate an aircraft in a careless or reckless manner so as to endanger the life or property of another person. This is one of the most broadly applicable FARs and covers many unsafe behaviors.

questions.q: A pilot receives a Notice to Comply from the FAA regarding an alleged violation. What is the best first step?
choices: Ignore it — verbal notices have no legal force
Consult an aviation attorney familiar with FAR enforcement before responding
Immediately admit fault and accept any penalty
questions.explanation: FAA enforcement actions have serious consequences including certificate suspension or revocation. Consulting an aviation attorney (or the AOPA Legal Services Plan) before responding to any FAA violation notice is strongly recommended.

questions.q: What is the minimum flight visibility for VFR flight in Class E airspace below 10,000 ft MSL?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.155: The minimum flight visibility for VFR flight in Class E airspace below 10,000 ft MSL is 3 statute miles.

questions.q: When must a pilot report a change of address to the FAA Civil Aviation Registry?
choices: Within 30 days of the address change
Within 60 days of the address change
Only when requesting a new certificate
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.60: A certificated pilot may not, after 30 days from a change in permanent mailing address, exercise the privileges of the certificate unless the holder has notified the FAA Airmen Certification Branch in writing.

questions.q: Under what conditions may a private pilot act as PIC of an aircraft for a charitable organization?
choices: Any time the organization is a registered nonprofit
Only under specific conditions outlined in 14 CFR 61.113(d) — charity flights operated under a specific FAA exception, typically requiring advance DOT/FAA authorization
Never — private pilots cannot fly for any charitable purpose
Anytime the pilot charges nothing personally
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 61.113(d): A private pilot may act as PIC of an aircraft in connection with a business or employment if the flight is only incidentally related to business and is not a primary business function. Charitable flights for compensation are a complex area requiring specific FAA authorization.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the NASA Aviation Safety Reporting System (ASRS)?
choices: To report accidents to the NTSB
A confidential voluntary reporting system for aviation incidents and near-misses — filing a report within 10 days provides limited immunity from FAA certificate action for inadvertent violations
To report mechanical defects to aircraft manufacturers
Required by FAA for all commercial operators
questions.explanation: The NASA ASRS (AIM 7-6-1): A voluntary confidential reporting system that provides limited immunity from FAA enforcement for inadvertent violations IF a report is filed within 10 days of the incident. Does not protect from criminal violations or accidents.

questions.q: What is an Airworthiness Certificate and when does it expire?
choices: Issued each year by the FAA — expires annually
Issued when the aircraft is manufactured; valid as long as the aircraft is maintained in accordance with FARs and has not been modified beyond its type certificate
Must be renewed every time the aircraft changes ownership
questions.explanation: An Airworthiness Certificate is issued at the time of manufacture and is valid indefinitely as long as the aircraft is maintained in accordance with applicable FARs and has not been modified in a way that voids the type certificate.

questions.q: What color scheme identifies mandatory instruction signs at airports?
questions.explanation: Mandatory instruction signs have white inscriptions on a red background. They identify areas where entry is restricted or requires ATC clearance — runway designations at hold-short lines, no-entry signs, and ILS critical area boundaries.

questions.q: A yellow inscription on a black background at an airport indicates what type of sign?
choices: A mandatory instruction sign requiring immediate stop
A location sign telling the pilot where they are (taxiway or runway designation)
A direction sign pointing toward a runway
A closed runway or taxiway marker
questions.explanation: Yellow inscription on black background = location sign. It identifies the current taxiway or runway the aircraft is on. Black inscription on yellow background = direction/destination sign showing where to go.

questions.q: What do the four yellow lines across a taxiway (two solid, two dashed) indicate, and which side must you stop on?
choices: Pavement edge — stop on either side
Runway hold-short position — stop on the SOLID line side. Never cross without ATC clearance.
Instrument landing system critical area — proceed when the path is clear
Crossing zone — yield to aircraft on the runway
questions.explanation: Hold-short lines consist of four yellow lines: two solid (on the side away from the runway) and two dashed (on the runway side). Stop on the solid side. The solid lines always face the direction traffic must hold short. Never cross without an explicit ATC clearance.

questions.q: What does a VASI showing red over white indicate?
choices: Too low — danger of terrain or obstacle contact
questions.explanation: VASI color combinations: white over white = too high; red over white = on glidepath ('red over white, you're alright'); red over red = too low and dangerous. Remember: two reds means you're headed for the ground.

questions.q: What does a PAPI showing 4 red lights indicate?
choices: Normal glidepath — all red is correct on PAPI
Slightly high — add a small amount of descent
Too low — dangerously below the glidepath, immediate corrective action required
questions.explanation: PAPI (4 lights): 4 white = too high; 3 white/1 red = slightly high; 2 white/2 red = on glidepath; 1 white/3 red = slightly low; 4 red = dangerously too low. Immediate power and pitch correction required at 4 red.

questions.q: What color are taxiway edge lights and taxiway centerline lights?
choices: White edge lights and blue centerline lights
Blue edge lights and green centerline lights
Green edge lights and white centerline lights
Yellow edge lights and blue centerline lights
questions.explanation: Taxiway edge lights are blue. Taxiway centerline lights are green. Runway edge lights are white (with yellow in the last 2,000 ft or last half of runway if shorter than 4,000 ft). This color coding helps distinguish taxiways from runways at night.

questions.q: What airport beacon configuration indicates a civilian land airport?
questions.explanation: Civilian land airport: alternating white and green flashes. Military airport: two white flashes alternating with one green. Water airport: white and yellow. Beacon operating during the day may indicate weather below VFR minimums.

questions.q: What is a hot spot on an airport diagram?
choices: A location where turbulence is commonly reported
A location on the airport movement area with a history of incidents or potential for confusion — depicted as numbered circles on the airport diagram
An area where the pavement is weak and may cause tire damage
A taxiway that can only be used in one direction
questions.explanation: Hot spots are labeled HS-1, HS-2, etc. on airport diagrams and FAA charts. They identify locations with complex geometry, sight line issues, or a history of runway incursions. Descriptions are in the Chart Supplement. Extra vigilance required.

questions.q: What is the correct three-step procedure for transferring flight controls between pilots?
choices: The receiving pilot simply takes the controls when ready
Giving pilot: 'You have the controls.' Receiving pilot: 'I have the controls.' (takes physical control) Giving pilot: 'You have the controls.' (confirms transfer)
A nod or hand gesture is sufficient — verbal exchange is not required
Only required for instrument flight — not necessary for VFR flight
questions.explanation: Positive exchange of controls uses a specific three-step verbal and physical exchange to prevent both pilots from thinking the other has the controls (or neither does). This exact phraseology is the FAA standard and should be used every time controls are transferred between any two people.

questions.q: What is CFIT and why is it significant in general aviation?
choices: Controlled Flight Into Terrain — an accident where a functioning aircraft is flown into terrain or water without crew awareness. One of the leading causes of fatal GA accidents, typically associated with VFR flight into IMC or loss of situational awareness.

Crew-initiated Flight Instrument Transition — a technique where the pilot deliberately shifts from visual to instrument references during VFR flight, required when visibility deteriorates, helping prevent spatial disorientation and terrain contact.

Certified Flight Into Terrain rating — an advanced endorsement required before pilots may legally conduct low-level flights below 500 ft AGL for agricultural operations, powerline patrol, or aerial survey missions under 14 CFR Part 137.

Controlled Flight Instrument Training — a recurrent proficiency program requiring pilots to log six hours of simulated IMC each 24-month period, intended to maintain scan technique and reduce inadvertent VFR-into-IMC accident rates.
questions.explanation: CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) occurs when a properly functioning aircraft is inadvertently flown into terrain, water, or obstacles. Causes include: VFR into IMC, spatial disorientation, poor situational awareness, and terrain avoidance complacency. Use terrain awareness tools, maintain SA, and always have an out when flying near terrain.
